question: Explain the core differences between the Collection and Map interfaces in the Java Collections Framework. Why are they considered separate root interfaces?
expectedPoints: Collection is a single-value interface for storing groups of objects (e.g., List, Set, Queue).
Map is a two-value (key-value pair) interface for storing associative arrays; it does not extend the Collection interface.
They are separate root interfaces because their fundamental data models are incompatible; a Collection contains elements, while a Map contains mappings.

question: Describe the primary purpose and key characteristics of the java.util.Set interface. Name two of its implementations and explain a key difference between them.
expectedPoints: The primary purpose of Set is to model a mathematical set, ensuring no duplicate elements.
Key characteristics: it contains no pair of elements e1 and e2 such that e1.equals(e2), and it allows at most one null element.
Example implementations: HashSet (unordered, uses hashCode()) and TreeSet (sorted, uses Comparable/Comparator).
Key difference: HashSet offers constant-time performance for basic operations but does not maintain order, while TreeSet guarantees elements will be sorted but offers log(n) time cost.

question: What is the fundamental contract between the equals() and hashCode() methods when using objects as keys in a HashMap?
expectedPoints: If two objects are equal according to the equals() method, then calling the hashCode() method on each of the two objects must produce the same integer result.
It is not required that unequal objects have different hash codes, but generating distinct hash codes for unequal objects can improve the performance of hash-based collections.
